edges.justification: Real yields represent the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding gold; when real yields rise, TIPS offer a guaranteed positive real return, making gold less attractive, and vice versa. This is one of the most well-documented causal relationships in gold-price modeling.
The Fed Funds rate raises the return on cash and money-market instruments relative to gold, directly affecting gold's opportunity cost and thus its price.
The Fed Funds rate anchors the short end of the yield curve and transmits to longer-dated real yields through the expectations channel and monetary-policy signaling.

Monetary policy settings influence market inflation expectations; a tighter Fed Funds rate signals a credible anti-inflation stance, compressing break-even inflation, while easing raises inflation expectations.
Break-even inflation captures market forward inflation expectations; when long-run inflation expectations rise, investors demand gold as an inflation hedge, pushing its price higher.

By construction, the 10-year break-even equals the nominal yield minus the real TIPS yield, so movements in break-even inflation are arithmetically and mechanically linked to real yields given a nominal yield level.
Realized CPI inflation represents actual purchasing-power erosion of fiat currency, triggering inflation-hedge demand for gold as investors seek to preserve real wealth.
Realized inflation outcomes update market participants' inflation expectations, causing break-even inflation rates to adjust as investors revise their forward-looking inflation pricing.
The Fed's price-stability mandate means rising realized CPI inflation causally prompts FOMC tightening (raising the Fed Funds rate), while falling inflation encourages easing.
Higher realized inflation can compress or shift real yields as it feeds into nominal yield adjustments and TIPS breakeven pricing; persistent inflation surprises alter the real yield level.

Gold is priced in U.S. dollars globally; a stronger dollar makes gold more expensive for foreign-currency holders, reducing global demand and depressing the dollar-denominated price, with the reverse for dollar weakness.

Oil is also dollar-denominated globally, so dollar strength raises the effective cost of oil for foreign buyers, reducing demand and exerting downward pressure on oil prices, while dollar weakness supports oil prices.
Higher U.S. interest rates attract capital inflows seeking higher returns, increasing demand for dollars and appreciating the DXY; rate cuts reduce the dollar's yield advantage and weaken it.
Higher U.S. real yields make dollar-denominated assets more attractive to global investors, increasing capital inflows and supporting dollar strength.

The risk-on/risk-off dynamic means strong equity returns shift investor demand toward growth assets and away from defensive gold, while equity selloffs trigger capital rotation into gold as a safe haven.
Elevated VIX signals financial-market stress and investor risk aversion, driving safe-haven capital flows into gold as investors seek assets uncorrelated with equity drawdowns.
VIX spikes are mechanically and causally linked to equity selloffs; rising implied volatility reflects and reinforces negative equity returns through options pricing and investor de-risking behavior.

Widening credit spreads signal rising systemic financial stress, triggering safe-haven demand for gold as investors seek assets with no counterparty risk and no exposure to the corporate credit system.
Credit spread widening indicates deteriorating corporate financial conditions, which typically coincides with and reinforces equity market declines as the same risk-off dynamic affects both markets.
Credit stress and equity volatility are jointly driven by financial-system risk aversion; widening spreads and rising VIX reflect and reinforce each other during periods of financial distress.

Rising debt-to-GDP signals increased long-run fiscal sustainability risk and currency-debasement probability, driving demand for gold as a hedge against sovereign-credit deterioration and fiat-currency weakness.
Higher government debt burdens raise the perceived probability of future inflationary debt monetization, pushing up market-implied long-run inflation expectations embedded in break-even rates.

Deteriorating fiscal sustainability can spill over into broader credit-market stress, as higher sovereign debt loads raise systemic risk perceptions and widen corporate credit spreads through contagion.

Oil prices influence gold through two channels: energy is a major input cost in gold mining, so higher oil prices raise production costs; and oil price increases feed through to consumer-price inflation, stimulating inflation-hedge demand for gold.
Energy prices are a direct component of the CPI basket and also raise transportation and production costs across the economy, so oil price increases causally drive realized consumer-price inflation.
Oil price movements shift market participants' forward inflation expectations since energy is a major driver of CPI; rising oil prices tend to push up 10-year break-even inflation rates.

The global business cycle captured by the CLI affects gold through the safe-haven demand channel: below-trend global activity raises recession fears and defensive demand for gold, while above-trend expansion rotates capital toward growth assets.

Equity markets are forward-looking and respond to leading indicators of global economic activity; a rising CLI signals improving global conditions that support corporate earnings expectations and equity returns.
Global economic activity is the primary driver of oil demand; a rising CLI signals stronger industrial output and transportation activity, which increases oil consumption and supports oil prices.
Improving global economic conditions reduce corporate default risk, compressing credit spreads; deteriorating CLI signals rising recession probability and wider credit spreads.
Mine production is the primary physical supply flow in the gold market; higher mine output increases available supply and exerts downward pressure on gold prices, holding demand constant.
Central bank net purchases represent official-sector physical demand; net buying removes gold from available market supply and adds to total demand, directly supporting higher prices.

ETF inflows require physical gold to be vaulted to back new shares, directly removing supply from the market; net outflows release physical gold back, representing a direct investment-demand channel with immediate price impact.

Jewelry consumption is the largest single category of gold demand; higher jewelry purchases increase total physical demand and support gold prices, while high gold prices inversely suppress price-sensitive jewelry buying.

Gold price levels causally affect jewelry demand in price-sensitive consumer markets (India, China); high gold prices reduce affordability and suppress jewelry consumption, creating a feedback from price to demand.

Rising gold prices attract momentum-driven and return-chasing institutional investors into gold ETFs, while falling prices trigger outflows; price levels influence investor sentiment and thus ETF flow direction.
Sustained higher gold prices improve mine economics and incentivize production expansion over medium-to-long horizons, though the supply response is slow due to multi-year development lead times.
Geopolitical risk events trigger flight-to-safety demand for gold as investors and central banks seek tangible assets with no counterparty risk when international tensions escalate.
Geopolitical risk events raise financial-market uncertainty and investor risk aversion, contributing to VIX spikes as equity-market participants price in higher near-term volatility risk.

Geopolitical tensions — particularly in oil-producing regions — disrupt supply expectations and trigger risk premia in oil markets, directly affecting oil prices through the supply-disruption channel.

Geopolitical risk events create uncertainty about global economic conditions and corporate earnings, typically weighing on equity returns as investors de-risk portfolios during periods of elevated international tensions.

Geopolitical risk — particularly the risk of sanctions on FX reserves as illustrated by post-2022 Russian reserve freezes — has directly motivated central bank diversification into gold, driving net purchases.

Economic policy uncertainty raises the risk premium on policy-exposed assets and drives demand for gold as a counterparty-risk-free asset unaffected by any single policy regime, supporting higher gold prices.
Policy uncertainty contributes to equity-market volatility; elevated EPU reflects conditions under which investors demand higher option prices to hedge uncertain market outcomes, pushing VIX higher.
High economic policy uncertainty depresses equity valuations by raising discount rates and compressing earnings expectations, weighing on S&P 500 returns.
Policy uncertainty raises credit-market risk premia as investors become less confident about the regulatory and fiscal environment, contributing to wider investment-grade credit spreads.

Policy uncertainty drives institutional investors into gold ETFs as a hedge against policy-regime risk; elevated EPU is associated with positive ETF inflows as fund managers seek gold's policy-neutral characteristics.
Consumer pessimism about sustained multi-year bad economic times raises behavioral demand for defensive stores of value including retail gold purchases, coins, and bars, supporting gold prices.
Consumer pessimism about future economic conditions signals weak household spending expectations, which weighs on corporate earnings outlooks and depresses equity returns.
When U.S. consumers expect sustained bad times economically, discretionary spending including gold jewelry purchases declines, reducing jewelry demand.

Silver and gold share overlapping demand drivers — inflation expectations, safe-haven demand, monetary debasement concerns — so shocks to precious-metals sentiment simultaneously affect both prices through shared investor demand channels.

Silver's large industrial-demand component (~50% of total) makes it sensitive to global economic conditions; equity returns and silver returns share common industrial-activity drivers that create a causal linkage through the business cycle.

Rising unemployment signals economic weakness, triggering both monetary-policy easing (which lowers opportunity costs for gold) and increased safe-haven demand as recession risk rises, both supporting higher gold prices.

The Fed's maximum-employment mandate means rising unemployment directly causally prompts FOMC easing (lower Fed Funds rate), while falling unemployment toward full employment gives the Fed room or reason to tighten.

Rising unemployment signals deteriorating economic conditions and corporate earnings, weighing on equity valuations and returns; improving labor markets support earnings growth and equity performance.

Labor-market slack (high unemployment) reduces wage growth and consumer spending, exerting downward pressure on consumer prices through the Phillips-curve mechanism; tight labor markets do the reverse.

Rising unemployment signals economic deterioration and increases corporate default risk expectations, causally widening credit spreads as investors demand greater compensation for holding corporate debt.
Rising unemployment directly increases consumer pessimism about future multi-year economic conditions as households observe job losses and rising economic insecurity in their communities.
Higher unemployment reduces household disposable income and consumer confidence, directly depressing discretionary spending including jewelry purchases.
The OECD CLI leads turning points in global economic activity; below-trend global growth (CLI falling below 100) leads to labor-market deterioration and rising unemployment with a lag.
Global economic expansion supports rising household incomes particularly in key jewelry-demand markets like India and China, driving higher consumer spending on gold jewelry.
Above-trend global economic activity tightens product and labor markets, creating inflationary pressures that feed through to realized CPI inflation; below-trend activity does the reverse.

Geopolitical risk events raise systemic uncertainty and can disrupt global supply chains and corporate revenue streams, widening credit spreads as investors demand higher compensation for corporate credit risk.

Major geopolitical events create domestic policy uncertainty — triggering debates about sanctions, defense spending, trade policy, and fiscal response — causing EPU to rise alongside GPR during international crises.
Higher government debt burdens can raise term premia on long-dated Treasuries as investors demand greater compensation for fiscal risk, pushing up nominal and real yields on 10-year securities.

Economic policy uncertainty contributes to consumer pessimism about future economic conditions; when households observe policy confusion or fiscal instability, their expectations about the next five years worsen.

Both silver and oil have significant industrial demand components and respond to global economic activity; strong industrial output drives simultaneous demand for both commodities through the industrial-activity channel.
